title: Applying wax resist for glaze decoration
subtitle: Reserving areas of bisqueware from glaze using liquid wax emulsion
excerpt: Wax resist is applied to bisqueware before glazing to keep specific areas glaze-free. The wax repels liquid glaze; where wax has been applied, the clay surface stays bare. In the kiln firing, the wax burns away cleanly. The technique is used to keep the foot ring clean, create geometric patterns, or mask areas for two-colour glaze effects.
sourceNotes: Wax resist glaze technique derived from first principles.

attrs.body: Glazes contain metal oxides; do not ingest or sand dry glaze. Wax resist burns off in the kiln and produces brief fumes; ensure kiln ventilation during the early stages of glaze firing. Dedicated brushes must be used for wax resist; wax ruins brushes for other uses. Do not use food-contact vessels unless glaze is rated food-safe and fired to the manufacturer's specified cone.
attrs.title: Kiln and glaze safety

content.text: Liquid
wax resist
is water-based and applied with a brush to
bisqueware

before glazing. The wax dries quickly and repels the liquid glaze when brushed or dipped over it. The most common use is waxing the foot ring to keep it bare so the pot does not stick to the kiln shelf during glaze firing. The same principle applies to any decorative area intended to stay unglazed.
Types of wax resist

Liquid wax emulsion (water-based) is the standard studio product; it cleans up with water before drying and thins with water. Hot paraffin wax is an alternative but requires melting equipment and is harder to control. Water-based resist is the better choice for precise line work.
Preparing the bisqueware

Wipe the bisqueware surface with a slightly damp sponge to remove dust from the kiln. Do not wet the surface with water; excess moisture causes the wax to bead rather than soak in. The bisqueware surface should be clean and dry to the touch.
Applying the wax
1. Waxing the foot ring

Place the piece on a banding wheel. Load a dedicated brush with resist and paint it onto the foot ring surface: the standing ring and a short distance up the inner and outer sides. Apply two coats for full coverage. Keep the brush flat against the foot surface rather than pressing into crevices; wax inside the foot ring recessed area does not affect the glaze result.
2. Decorative wax work

For decorative resist patterns, use a liner brush to paint geometric shapes, stripes, or freehand marks onto the glazed or unglazed bisqueware surface. Wax can be applied over a dried first glaze coat; the second glaze colour then sits on top or is wiped away from the waxed area.
3. Let the wax dry before glazing
Allow the wax to dry for a minimum of 20 minutes before applying glaze. Glazing over wet wax emulsion causes
crawling
in the firing. Dried wax feels slightly tacky; fully dry wax feels smooth and slightly rubbery.
Glazing over wax

Apply glaze by dipping, pouring, or brushing. Where the wax is, the glaze will bead off and leave the bisque surface bare. If the glaze is brushed over wax, use light strokes; pressing hard can force glaze under the wax edge. Wax-covered areas may look slightly greasy under glaze; this is normal and the wax burns away in the kiln.
What happens in the kiln

The wax burns off between approximately 200°C and 400°C during the glaze firing, leaving the bare bisqueware surface beneath. The glaze on either side of the wax edge fires normally. The fired result shows a clean hard edge between glazed and unglazed areas.

items.fix: Wait at least 20-30 minutes after applying wax before glazing; in humid conditions, allow longer
items.cause: Glaze applied before wax was fully dry
items.symptom: Glaze crawls away from the waxed area in the firing

items.fix: Apply two coats of undiluted wax; do not thin with water
items.cause: Wax applied too thinly, or wax emulsion too diluted
items.symptom: Wax does not repel the glaze; glaze soaks in

items.fix: Use light brush strokes near wax edges; dipping produces the cleanest edge
items.cause: Glaze applied with too much pressure over the wax edge
items.symptom: Glaze seeps under the wax edge leaving a ragged line
